exercises.title: Explicit Type Conversions
exercises.description: Practice converting between Python types using int(), float(), str(), and bool().
exercises.starterCode:
# int() conversions
print(int("42"))        # string to int
print(int(3.9))         # float to int (truncates!)
print(int(True))        # bool to int
print(int(False))       # bool to int

# float() conversions
print(float("3.14"))    # string to float
print(float(7))         # int to float

# str() conversions
print(str(100))         # int to string
print(str(3.14))        # float to string
print(str(True))        # bool to string

# bool() conversions
print(bool(1))          # int to bool
print(bool(0))          # int to bool
hints: int() truncates the decimal part — it does NOT round.

exercises.title: String to Number for Calculations
exercises.description: Simulate what happens when data comes in as strings (like from a file or web form). Convert the string values to numbers to perform calculations.
exercises.starterCode:
# These values came in as strings (simulating real-world data)
price_str = "49.95"
quantity_str = "3"
discount_str = "10"   # percentage

# TODO: Convert to appropriate numeric types
price = float(price_str)
quantity = int(quantity_str)
discount_percent = int(discount_str)

# TODO: Calculate subtotal, discount amount, and final total
subtotal = price * quantity
discount_amount = subtotal * (discount_percent / 100)
total = subtotal - discount_amount

# TODO: Print formatted results
print(f"Subtotal:  ${subtotal:.2f}")
print(f"Discount:  ${discount_amount:.2f}")
print(f"Total:     ${total:.2f}")
hints: Use float() for price (has decimal), int() for whole numbers.
discount_percent / 100 converts the percentage to a decimal multiplier.

exercises.title: Type Checking with isinstance()
exercises.description: isinstance() checks if a value is of a certain type. Use it to print True or False for various type checks.
exercises.starterCode:
values = [42, 3.14, "hello", True, None]

# TODO: For each value, print isinstance() checks
print(isinstance(42, int))
print(isinstance(3.14, float))
print(isinstance("hello", str))
print(isinstance(True, bool))
print(isinstance(True, int))   # bool IS a subclass of int
print(isinstance(None, type(None)))

# TODO: Print the types of each value using type()
for v in values:
    print(type(v))
exercises.expectedOutput:
True
True
True
True
True
True
<class 'int'>
<class 'float'>
<class 'str'>
<class 'bool'>
<class 'NoneType'>
hints: isinstance(True, int) is True because bool is a subtype of int.
type(None) is NoneType — Python's special type for the None value.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Type Conversion Basics
warmup.description: int() converts to whole number (truncates), float() adds decimal, str() converts to text. What does this print?
warmup.starterCode:
print(int("100"))
print(float("2.5"))
print(str(42))
print(int(7.9))    # truncates, does NOT round
